返回本批次

研究：“安全疲劳”可能会削弱数字防御

Study: 'Security Fatigue' May Weaken Digital Defenses

www.albany.edu 作者 giuliomagnifico 分数 85 评论 58 2026/03/23 22:36
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 加州大学伯克利分校领导的一个研究小组发现，反复接触安全要求可能会导致“安全疲劳”，在这种状态下，员工会变得精神疲惫并脱离安全实践。 （帕特里克·多德森摄）作者：Michael Parker 纽约州奥尔巴尼（2026 年 3 月 18 日）——从密码重置和软件更新到网络钓鱼警报和网络安全培训，当今的工作场所充满了有关数字安全的不断提醒。但奥尔巴尼大学马斯里商学院领导的新研究表明，这些善意的保护措施可能会产生意想不到的效果。 最近发表在《欧洲信息系统杂志》上的一项研究《安全疲劳：自我调节能力耗尽导致的情绪疲惫和愤世嫉俗》研究了不断增长的网络安全需求如何影响员工行为。研究发现，反复接触安全要求可能会导致“安全疲劳”，即员工精神疲惫并脱离安全实践的状态。 “安全要求旨在保护组织，但随着时间的推移，它们也对员工提出了额外的要求，”莫里斯马斯里捐赠教授兼信息安全和数字取证主席 Sanjay Goel 说。 “当这些要求超出了个人的管理能力时，保持一致的安全行为就变得更加困难。”Goel 与伦敦城市大学（英国）市政厅商法学院的 Akanksha Malik 和印度德里印度理工学院（印度）的 Shuchi Sinha 共同进行了这项研究。网络安全的人性化一面虽然网络安全通常被认为是一项技术挑战，但该研究突显了必须实时执行安全实践的员工所面临的日益增长的压力。
  2. Morris Massry 教授兼信息安全和数字取证主席 Sanjay Goel 在 UAlbany 商业人工智能实验室向学生发表演讲。 （帕特里克·多德森摄）
  3. 员工通常需要管理一系列与安全相关的任务，从维护复杂的密码到识别网络钓鱼尝试以及适应频繁更新的策略。虽然每项任务本身都是可以管理的，但累积效应可能会产生持续的认知负担，干扰员工的主要职责。随着时间的推移，这种负担会耗尽个人的自我调节能力，导致疲劳，表现为情绪疲惫和脱离安全实践。当这种情况发生时，员工可能会开始忽视警告，重复使用弱密码或寻求让他们保持生产力的变通办法。“人们并没有试图绕过安全性，”戈埃尔说。 “在许多情况下，他们只是被要求的数量和复杂性压垮了。”研究发现，这种行为通常不是恶意的，而是超负荷的结果。不敬业的员工以前通常很顺从，但由于不断的要求而疲惫不堪。减少疲劳，提高合规性为了更好地了解这个问题，研究人员调查了近 300 名具有组织网络安全政策经验的全职美国员工。他们的研究结果表明，当安全要求干扰员工完成主要工作职责的能力时，最有可能出现安全疲劳。同时，该研究还确定了组织可以降低风险的方法。对自己管理安全任务的能力（称为安全自我效能）充满信心的员工以及了解网络安全风险的员工更有可能保持合规性，即使存在疲劳。“组织需要仔细考虑如何实施安全策略，”戈埃尔说。 “提供培训、简化流程并将安全性集成到日常工作流程中可以减少摩擦并帮助员工持续参与安全实践。”该研究强调了组织支持（包括培训和技术援助）在帮助员工更有效地满足安全要求方面的作用。当这些支持到位时，安全需求对日常工作的影响就会减少。随着网络威胁的不断发展，研究结果指出了一个更广泛的挑战：加强安全性，同时又不会压垮负责执行安全工作的人员。研究人员建议，组织不仅要关注更严格的政策，还要关注设计可持续的系统。

Original Summary

  1. A UAlbany-led research team found that repeated exposure to security requirements can lead to “security fatigue,” a state in which employees become mentally exhausted and disengaged from security practices. (Photo by Patrick Dodson)By Michael ParkerALBANY, N.Y. (March 18, 2026) — From password resets and software updates to phishing alerts and cybersecurity trainings, today’s workplace is filled with constant reminders about digital security. But new research led by the University at Albany’s Massry School of Business suggests those well-intentioned safeguards may be having an unintended effect.A recent study, “Security Fatigue: Manifestation of Emotional Exhaustion and Cynicism by Depletion of Self-Regulation Capacity,” published in the European Journal of Information Systems, examines how growing cybersecurity demands are impacting employee behavior. The research finds that repeated exposure to security requirements can lead to “security fatigue,” a state in which employees become mentally exhausted and disengaged from security practices. “Security requirements are designed to protect organizations, but they also create additional demands on employees that build over time,” said Sanjay Goel, Morris Massry Endowed Professor and chair of Information Security and Digital Forensics. “When those demands outpace an individual’s capacity to manage them, it becomes harder to maintain consistent security behavior.”Goel conducted the research with co-authors Akanksha Malik of the Guildhall School of Business and Law at London Metropolitan University (UK) and Shuchi Sinha of the Indian Institute of Technology Delhi (India).The human side of cybersecurityWhile cybersecurity is often framed as a technical challenge, the study highlights the growing strain placed on employees who must carry out security practices in real time.
  2. Morris Massry Endowed Professor and Chair, Information Security and Digital Forensics Sanjay Goel speaks to students in the UAlbany AI for Business Lab. (Photo by Patrick Dodson)
  3. Employees are routinely expected to manage a range of security-related tasks, from maintaining complex passwords to identifying phishing attempts and adapting to frequently updated policies. While each task is manageable on its own, the cumulative effect can create a sustained cognitive burden that interferes with employees’ primary responsibilities.Over time, that burden depletes an individual’s ability to self-regulate, leading to fatigue that manifests as emotional exhaustion and disengagement from security practices. When that happens, employees may begin to ignore warnings, reuse weak passwords or seek workarounds that allow them to stay productive.“People aren’t trying to bypass security,” Goel said. “In many cases, they’re simply overwhelmed by the volume and complexity of what’s being asked of them.”The study finds that this behavior is typically not malicious, but the result of overload. Employees who disengage were often previously compliant but have been worn down by constant demands.Reducing fatigue, improving complianceTo better understand the issue, the researchers surveyed nearly 300 full-time U.S. employees with experience navigating organizational cybersecurity policies. Their findings show that security fatigue is most likely to develop when security requirements interfere with employees’ ability to complete their primary job duties.At the same time, the research identifies ways organizations can reduce the risk. Employees who feel confident in their ability to manage security tasks — known as security self-efficacy — and those who understand cybersecurity risks are more likely to maintain compliance, even when fatigue is present.“Organizations need to think carefully about how security policies are implemented,” Goel said. “Providing training, simplifying processes and integrating security into everyday workflows can reduce friction and help employees stay engaged with security practices.”The study highlights the role of organizational support, including training and technical assistance, in helping employees navigate security requirements more effectively. When those supports are in place, the impact of security demands on day-to-day work is reduced.As cyber threats continue to evolve, the findings point to a broader challenge: strengthening security without overwhelming the people responsible for carrying it out. The researchers suggest that organizations focus not only on stricter policies, but on designing systems that are sustainable over time.

评论（默认中文）

compiler-guy 2026/03/23 23:30
我在过去几年中尤其在几个 FAANG 中看到了这种现象。事情变得如此之多，并且需要如此多的特殊权限，以至于现在人们会抢先请求系统或程序的权限。因为当他们知道是否需要它时，已经太晚了。

安全行业似乎没有人考虑再迈出一步的总体负担。每一个本身都很简单，但累积起来就会带来巨大的麻烦，因此人们寻找解决方法。

I have seen this phenomenon especially at a couple of FAANGs over the past couple of years. Things are getting locked down so much, and so many special permissions are required that now people ask for permissions to systems or procedures preemptively. Because by the time they know if they will need it or not, it's too late.

And no one in the security business seems to consider the overall burden of yet another step. Each of which is simple in by itself, but cumulatively they are a giant hassle, and so people look for workarounds.

在 HN 中查看
baby_souffle 2026/03/23 23:42
> 安全行业似乎没有人考虑再迈出一步的总体负担。每一个本身都很简单，但累积起来就会带来巨大的麻烦，因此人们寻找解决方法。

这是一个与时间一样古老的故事。 在之前的一次演出中，IT 出于……$原因取消了触摸 ID。大约 40% 的工程团队已经热衷于机械键盘，因此只需要一个人就可以“仅供参考，VIA 允许您对宏进行编程”。它是否像便利贴上的密码一样糟糕？不完全是，但我无法想象 Touch ID 会构成更大的威胁。

> And no one in the security business seems to consider the overall burden of yet another step. Each of which is simple in by itself, but cumulatively they are a giant hassle, and so people look for workarounds.

This is a tale as old as time. At a prior gig, IT took away touch ID for ... $reasons. ~40% of the engineering team was already big into mechanical keyboards so it only took one person to "just FYI, VIA allows you to program macros". Is it _as bad_ as password on a sticky note? Not quite but I can't imagine that touch ID was _more_ of a threat.

Yubikeys 的一个重要用例是能够模拟键盘并在触摸时生成一串字符。
A big use case for Yubikeys is the ability to emulate a keyboard and produce a string of chars on touch.

我最讨厌的事。前五名之一是假的。服务器消息中有一个拼写错误。
My pet peeves. One of the top 5 was fake. It had a typo in the server message.

arcfour 2026/03/24 02:38
您知道有趣的是，至少在默认情况下，这些字符串中包含一些信息，可以告诉您密钥的序列号和型号等。
You know what's funny is that, at least by default, these strings have some information in them that tells you the serial number and model of the key, among other things.

klooney 2026/03/24 00:56

JasperNoboxdev 2026/03/24 00:01
好奇，为什么要删除 Touch ID？将所有内容都移入其中似乎是便利性和安全性的完美结合（特别是如果替代方案是将密钥复制到人工智能中:)）
Curious, why remove Touch ID? Been moving everything into it seems like a really good mix of convenience + security (especially if the alternative is copying your key into AI :) )
在 HN 中查看
SAI_Peregrinus 2026/03/24 00:14
我将这种事情称为自我 DoS。如果系统足够无法使用，则与 DoS 攻击没有什么区别。这种破坏不仅限于安全团队，任何导致系统不够可靠（从糟糕的设计到糟糕的性能）的行为都可能与外部攻击产生相同的效果。
I call this sort of thing a self-DoS. If the system is unusable enough, it's indistinguishable from a DoS attack. This sort of sabotage isn't restricted to the security team, anything that makes the system unreliable enough from bad design through bad performance can have the same effects as an external attack.
在 HN 中查看
burningChrome 2026/03/24 01:44
>> 事情变得如此之多，并且需要如此多的特殊权限，以至于现在人们会抢先请求系统或程序的权限。

目前我们现在的公司正在处理这个问题。人们强烈要求获得各种法学硕士学位。他们的采用速度很慢，而且由于我们是微软的大客户，我们只获得了有限的副驾驶许可证。然后，越来越多的人要求获得访问权限，他们慢慢地申请了大量的许可证，直到一小部分人最终获得了访问权限。

然后出现了人们想要插入副驾驶的所有其他非微软应用程序（例如Figma），这是用户的另一轮挫败感，因为他们锁定了一些东西，然后慢慢软化。

该公司仍在努力提供人工智能工具和法学硕士，因为现在该公司 真的 落后于许多其他刚刚在人工智能领域全面开放的公司。

我们正在 100% 处理您所说的内容。 EIS 一直让人们经历了如此多的障碍，以至于每次他们添加法学硕士时，它都完全锁定在企业网络中，人们感到非常沮丧，因为我们很多人已经在家里和其他地方很好地使用人工智能。然而，在这里，我们使用人工智能的日常工作是国会的一项法案，旨在获得法学硕士和工具。

>> Things are getting locked down so much, and so many special permissions are required that now people ask for permissions to systems or procedures preemptively.

Currently dealing with this at our current company. People were clamoring for access to various LLM's. They were slow to adopt and since we're a huge MS client, we were granted limited licenses for copilot. Then more people made waves about getting access and they slow walked a ton of licenses until a small portion finally had access.

Then came all the other non-MS apps that people wanted to plug copilot into (such as Figma) and that was another round of frustrations with users here as they locked stuff down, then slowly relented.

The company is still struggling with giving access to AI tools and LLM's since now the company is really lagging behind many other companies who are just running wide open with AI.

We're 100% dealing with what you're saying. EIS has been making people jump through so many hoops that every time they add an LLM, its completely locked down to just the enterprise network and people are getting really frustrated since so many of us are already well along using AI at home and elsewhere. Yet here our day-to-day stuff using AI is an act of congress to get access to the LLM and tools.

在 HN 中查看
whynotmaybe 2026/03/23 23:47
并不是很新。很久以前，我必须等待两个月才能访问开发服务器上的共享文件夹。

这种情况变得如此普遍，以至于每当我们计划任何事情时，如果某项任务必须由我们团队之外的人来完成，我们就会增加 20 天。

我猜是永恒的安全？

Not really new. A long time ago I had to wait 2 months to have access to a shared folder on a development server.

It became so prevalent that whenever we were planning anything, if a task had to be done by someone outside of our team, we added 20 days.

Security through eternity I guess ?

在 HN 中查看
alexsmirnov 2026/03/24 01:36
与我在一家大型医疗保健提供商工作的经历相比，几乎是瞬间……我等了 6 个月，IT 部门才允许我在工作笔记本电脑上安装开发工具。

虽然安全规则为工作设置了巨大的障碍，但乳清也留下了足够的漏洞可供利用。在获得所需的权限之前，我设法使用 Linux 创建双启动并在“批准”和“非法”系统之间共享文件

Almost instantly, compared to my experience working for a big health care provider... I waited 6 moths for IT department to allow me install development tools on work laptop.

And while security rules created enormous roadblocks for work, whey also left enough holes to be exploited. Before getting required permissions, I managed to create dual boot with linux and share files between 'approved' and 'illegal' systems

在 HN 中查看
arcfour 2026/03/24 02:34
> 安全行业似乎没有人考虑再迈出一步的总体负担。每一个本身都很简单，但累积起来就会带来巨大的麻烦，因此人们寻找解决方法。

这当然不是真的。我个人认为这会给用户带来多少摩擦，比如标准化必须重新输入密码太多，从而使网络钓鱼变得更容易，等等。众所周知，如果您以正确的方式做事变得太困难，那么您将获得影子 IT，情况会更糟。我经常提倡简化流程和程序，引入更多用户友好的系统，举办办公时间，让安全团队可以解答您的任何问题或疑虑，使我们更容易为公司服务，等等。

有什么问题吗？嗯，首先，这个领域有很多无能的人，所以他们会尽一切努力让自己看起来像是在工作。第二，大多数安全部门人手严重不足，所以即使你有能干的人，他们也只能快速把事情整理好，而无法清理干净。第三，大量愚蠢的监管和法律要求需要永远现代化。最后，一半的安全人员正在玩弄政治并与公司其他部门争论，这意味着您获得的解决方案有一半的时间是妥协的结果，没有人会满意。

TL;DR，我们不是没有同理心的精神病患者，我们为同样的原因而奋斗，就像你们开发人员有技术债务和其他糟糕的事情一样，尽管你们不愿意。

> And no one in the security business seems to consider the overall burden of yet another step. Each of which is simple in by itself, but cumulatively they are a giant hassle, and so people look for workarounds.

This is certainly not true. I personally consider how much friction things introduce for users, things like normalizing having to reenter your password too much making phishing easier, and so on. It's well understood that you will get shadow IT, which is worse, if you make doing things the right way too difficult. I regularly advocate for streamlining processes and procedures, introducing more user-friendly systems, hosting office hours where the security team is available for any question or concern you have making us more available to the company, etc.

What's the issue? Well, for one, there's a ton of incompetent people in the field, so they'll just do whatever to make themselves look like they're working. Two, most security departments are criminally understaffed, so even if you have competent people they just have to put things together quickly and can't clean it up. Three, there's tons of idiotic regulatory and legal requirements that take forever to modernize. And finally, half of security is playing politics and arguing with the rest of the company, meaning that half the time the solutions you get are a slop of compromise with which nobody is happy.

TL;DR we aren't psychopaths without empathy, we struggle for the same reasons you developers have tech debt and other things that suck even though you would prefer not to.

在 HN 中查看
onetimeusename 2026/03/23 23:59
我认为安全性成为合规性的一部分，因此安全性建议与实际安全性脱节。看起来很多安全建议只是繁忙的工作，这证明了拥有庞大的合规行业是合理的。因此，这方面的一个例子可能是代码的安全扫描器，其中输出甚至没有用处。但是，使用该工具来搜索不相关的结果是合规性所必需的，即使它基本上对安全性没有任何作用。
I think security became part of compliance so security recommendations got detached from actual security. It seems like a lot of security recommendations are just busy work that justifies having a huge compliance industry. So an example of this might be security scanners for code where the output is not even useful. But using the tool, which searches for irrelevant findings, is required for compliance even if it basically does nothing for security.

GoblinSlayer 2026/03/24 01:08
谁关注合规行业？
Who watches the compliance industry?

在 HN 中查看
dijit 2026/03/23 23:25
这就是 NIST 将密码轮换建议从 90 天更新为无限期的部分原因：如果太不方便，人们就会口头上强调安全性。你必须尝试在人们所在的地方去认识他们。

说教并不是长久以来的强大动力。

thats part of why NIST updated their password rotation recommendations from 90 days to indefinite: people pay lip service to security if it is too inconvenient. you have to try to meet people where they are.

Preaching is not a strong motivator for long.

在 HN 中查看
carefree-bob 2026/03/23 23:32
这不仅仅是为了“方便”，人类大脑很难记住真正随机的密码。您可以尝试所有您想要的助记技巧，但最终需要大量的时间和重复才能毫不费力地输入密码。所以人们所做的就是创建一系列可导出的密码。例如，我可以想到一句话“我喜欢在海洋上弹跳的沙滩球！”然后设置密码“ilBBbotocean！”当需要更改密码时，我只需添加一个数字“ilBBbotocean！1”。研究表明这就是人们所做的。但一旦链中的一个密码被泄露，攻击者也很容易获得这些密码。

其效果是，通过要求频繁轮换，该组织正在有效地培训其用户拥有单一永久密码并 永远不要改变它，即使在妥协之后。这是极其有害的。至少对于在数据库中出现或发生事件后强制轮换的永久密码，您在创建新密码方面的合规性要高得多，并且组织更安全，因为每个人都不会使用从以前的密码派生的密码。

It's not just about "convenience", it is hard for the human mind to remember a truly random password. You can try all the mnemonic tricks you want but at the end of the day it requires a lot of time and repetition before entering the password is effortless. So what people do is create a stream of derivable passwords. For example, I can think of a phrase "I love beach balls bouncing on the ocean!" and then make a password "ilBBbotocean!" and when it comes time to change that password, I'll just add a number "ilBBbotocean!1". Studies have shown this is what people do. But it is easy for attackers to also derive these passwords once one password in the chain has been compromised.

The effect of that is that by requiring frequent rotation, the organization is effectively training their users to have a single permanent password and to never change it, even after a compromise. That's extremely harmful. At least with permanent passwords that are force rotated after they show up in database or there has been an incident, you have a much higher percentage of compliance with making new passwords, and the organization is safer because everyone isn't using passwords derived from the previous password.

在 HN 中查看
mysteria 2026/03/23 23:40
我记得有一个案例，一家公司决定为员工分配随机的 16 个字符的密码（带符号），并每 90 天左右轮换一次。它们是不可更改的，其想法是每个人都将被迫使用定期更改的安全密码。

您可能会猜到发生了什么，那就是没有人记住他们的密码，而是人们将其写在便笺簿或便签纸上。

I remember a case where a company decided to assign employees random 16 character passwords with symbols and rotated them every 90 days or so. They were unchangeable and the idea was that everyone would be forced to use a secure password that changed regularly.

You can probably guess what happened, and that was that no one remembered their passwords and people wrote it down on their pads or sticky notes instead.

在 HN 中查看
bluGill 2026/03/24 00:15
写下密码是一个不错的选择。 但是，您需要将该文件保存在安全的位置。将其放入钱包中并将其视为 100 美元钞票 - 不要将其粘贴到显示器或键盘下方。

密码管理器对于大多数事情来说都更好，但您需要以某种方式解锁密码管理器。

Writing down a password is a great option. However you need to keep that paper in a secure location. Put it in your wallet and treat it like a $100 bill - don't paste it to a monitor or under the keyboard.

A password manager is better for most things, but you need to unlock the password manager somehow.

在 HN 中查看
GoblinSlayer 2026/03/24 00:10
还有“应用程序密码”。不仅可以更改，您甚至无法向其中添加文本。
Also "app passwords". Not just change, you can't even append text to it.
在 HN 中查看
SAI_Peregrinus 2026/03/24 00:22
这些只是人们可以输入的 API 键。
Those are just API keys people can type.
在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/03/24 00:14 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
nvgrw 2026/03/24 06:50
每次登录 FTB（加州税务机关）网站时，我都必须设置新密码。我希望有一些积极的指导来停止这样做，因为目前政府仍然认为强制更改密码会使其“更安全”。
Every time I log into the FTB (CA tax authority) website I have to set a new password. I wish there were some affirmative guidance to stop doing this because at the moment governments still think forcing password changes makes it “safer”.
在 HN 中查看
dragonwriter 2026/03/24 06:57
> 我希望有一些积极的指导来停止这样做，因为目前政府仍然认为强制更改密码会使其“更安全”。

NIST SP 800-63B-4 [0] 似乎是非常明确的“肯定性指导”，尽管它实际上只是合法的 必需的 在某些情况下。

[0] https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/sp800-63b.html @ 3.1.1.2：“[...]验证者和 CSP 不应要求订阅者定期更改密码。但是，如果有证据表明身份验证器已被泄露，验证者应强制更改密码。[...]”

> I wish there were some affirmative guidance to stop doing this because at the moment governments still think forcing password changes makes it “safer”.

NIST SP 800-63B-4 [0] seems to be pretty clear “affirmative guidance”, though its only actually legally required in certain circumstances.

[0] https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/sp800-63b.html @ 3.1.1.2: “[...] Verifiers and CSPs SHALL NOT require subscribers to change passwords periodically. However, verifiers SHALL force a change if there is evidence that the authenticator has been compromised. [...]”

在 HN 中查看
mystraline 2026/03/24 00:20
大多数联邦组织仍然有 60 天的密码轮换要求，尽管 NIST 大约 10 年前就给出了不要这样做的指导。

这意味着什么？密码存储在只能由管理员访问的纺织品中并共享。每个人都因此而变得更糟。

Most federal orgs still have 60 day password rotation requirements in place, even though NIST gave guidance almost 10 years ago not to do that.

What does that mean? Passwords are stored in textiles accessible by admin only, and shared. And everyone is worse for it.

在 HN 中查看
Joel_Mckay 2026/03/24 01:34
大多数情况下，它会强制死账户退出系统，因为裁员通知会在第二天发送。

这主要是为了确保一些忙碌的管理员不必清点每个用户的权限。

轮换域登录形成了与引导非活动用户类似的功能。

2FA 实际上可能会使系统变得更弱，因为人们可以使用虚假电信服务和密码重置以 23 美元的价格进行 MITM。 =3

Mostly, it forces dead accounts off the system, as lay off notices are sent the day after.

It is mostly about ensuring some busy admin doesn't have to inventory every user permission.

Rotating domain logins form a similar function of booting inactive users.

2FA actually may make a system weaker, as people can MITM for $23 using a bogus telecom service and password reset. =3

在 HN 中查看
dragonwriter 2026/03/24 07:02
> 这主要是为了确保一些忙碌的管理员不必清点每个用户的权限。

因此，这是一种糟糕的身份验证实践，它的维护是为了减轻不良身份验证实践的影响（您使身份验证 较少的 当人们打算获得授权时，希望比他们没有获得授权并且您没有清理他们的权限时更安全，密码过期将导致身份验证失败，因此他们的授权尚未被撤销这一事实并不重要），而不是采用良好的身份验证和身份验证实践？

> It is mostly about ensuring some busy admin doesn't have to inventory every user permission.

So, its a bad authn practice that is maintained to mitigate the impacts of bad authz practices (you make authn less secure when people are intended to be authorized, in the hopes than when they aren't and you haven't cleaned up their permissions, the password expiration will cause authn failures so the fact that their authorization hasn't been revoked won't matter), instead of adopting good authn and authz practices?

在 HN 中查看
Joel_Mckay 2026/03/24 08:22
出于安全原因，每个部门的资源通常都会在全球范围内同时与冗余员工抢先切断。 比小鸡啄每个用户组成员身份和批量密码失效要快得多。

如果前用户拥有 IT 管理和 VPN 访问权限，则需要时间来确定谁应该仍然存在。 轮换整个部门的访问权限以自动将非参与者踢出网络会更快。 然后清理特定用户登录，并将所有孤立用户资产迁移到部门共享中。

请记住，> 90% 的安全漏洞来自公司内部。 =3

Each departments resources are usually preemptively cutoff globally from the redundant employees at the same time for safety reasons. A lot faster than chicken pecking each users group membership, and batched password invalidation.

If the former user had IT administrative and VPN access, it would otherwise take time to figure out who should still be there. It is faster to rotate the whole departments access to auto kick non-participants off the network. Then mop up the specific user logins, and migrate any orphaned user assets into the department share.

Keep in mind >90% of security breaches come from within firms. =3

在 HN 中查看
MengerSponge 2026/03/24 03:22
SMS 2FA 是有害的。幸运的是，其他 2FA 模式也容易受到 MITM 攻击
SMS 2FA is harmful. Fortunately, other 2FA modalities are susceptible to that MITM attack
在 HN 中查看
donatj 2026/03/23 23:44
近年来的封锁程度非常严重。根据我们的 2FA 要求和 SSO，每天早上登录 GitHub 需要我花费大约八次点击和整整一分钟的时间。近年来，一切都变得如此封锁，人们正在努力保护基本的 CRUD 应用程序
The level of lockdown in current years is wild. With our 2FA requirements and SSO, signing into GitHub every morning takes me something like eight clicks and a solid minute. Everything has gotten so locked down in recent years, people are working so hard to protect what are largely basic CRUD apps
在 HN 中查看
anon7000 2026/03/24 08:12
有趣的是，我公司的 GitHub SSO 运行良好。他们使用 Okta。主帐户保持登录状态，但 SSO 帐户每天左右就会过期。但 Okta Fastpass 意味着流程是“点击登录，点击使用快速通行证，使用指纹进行 Touch ID”，这样你就成功了
Interesting, my company’s GitHub SSO works fine. They use Okta. The main account stays logged in, but the SSO account expires every day or so. But Okta Fastpass means the flow is “click login, click use fastpass, use fingerprint for Touch ID” and you’re golden
在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/03/24 00:10
只要您保持登录状态或者至少在早上成功进行身份验证后有一个简短的登录过程就可以了。

CRUD 应用程序可能包含非常敏感的数据，因此不确定这有何相关性。

That’s fine as long as you are kept logged in or at least have an abbreviated login process after successfully authenticating in the morning.

CRUD apps can contain very sensitive data, so not sure how that’s relevant.

在 HN 中查看
donatj 2026/03/24 03:45
我全力支持用我的生命来保护数据，但是围绕 CRUD 应用程序的代码的价值越来越小，而这正是我们保留在 GitHub 中的内容。
I'm all for protecting the data with my life, but there's increasingly little value in the code around a CRUD app, which is what we're keeping in GitHub.
在 HN 中查看
magicalhippo 2026/03/24 00:26
如果 GitHub 允许适当的 SSO 而不是这种混合帐户混合，情况会更少。

我知道混合方法对于承包商或类似的拥有很多帽子的人来说可能是理想的，但对于普通员工来说它只会增加摩擦而没有任何好处。

Would have been less if GitHub had just allowed proper SSO instead of this hybrid account mixing.

I get that the hybrid method might be desirable for contractors or similar who have many hats, but for a regular employee it just adds friction for no benefit.

在 HN 中查看
nightpool 2026/03/24 00:52
我在使用 Github 时从未遇到过这样的问题，我认为与我使用的其他 SSO 系统相比，他们的帐户混合设置将我登录所需的工作量减少了 100 倍。
I've never had that issue with Github—I think their account mixing setup reduces the amount of work I have to do to sign in 100x compared to other SSO systems I use.
在 HN 中查看
magicalhippo 2026/03/24 01:03
您一定使用过一些奇怪的其他 SSO 系统，这是我唯一的解释。

GitHub 拥有与我们使用的其他任何东西一样的所有正常 SSO 功能，但除了 GitHub 特定的帐户登录之外。在其他地方，我只是通过 SSO 登录，在 GitHub 中，我首先登录 GitHub（具有自己的 MFA），然后 然后 与其他地方相同的 SSO 步骤。

You must have used some weird other SSO systems is the only explanation I have.

GitHub has all the normal SSO stuff as anything else we use, but on top of the GitHub-specific account login. Everywhere else I just log in via SSO, in GitHub I log in first to GitHub (with its own MFA) and then the same SSO step as anywhere else.

在 HN 中查看
nightpool 2026/03/24 01:10
我从来不需要登录 Github 作为我日常流程的一部分。只需一次即可设置新计算机。您是否使用隐身窗口或其他方式登录？
I've never had to log in to Github as part of my daily flow. Only once to set up a new computer. Are you logging in using an incognito window or something?
在 HN 中查看
magicalhippo 2026/03/24 01:56
有趣的。也许是因为我不是每天使用 GitHub，我们正在迁移到 GitHub，所以我仍然在旧系统中的存储库中工作。另外，也许我受到的影响更大，因为我也在做组织管理工作。
Interesting. Perhaps it's because I'm not using GitHub daily, we're migrating to GitHub so I still do work in repos which live in the old system. Also, perhaps I'm more affected because I'm doing org admin stuff as well.
在 HN 中查看
charlieboardman 2026/03/24 00:58
我的 Steam 密码是我能记住的一个简短而奇怪的短语。从高中开始，大约15年前，我就没有改变过它。从来没有任何安全问题。

现代景观令人沮丧，因为这种设置确实有效。从技术角度来看，密码实际上很棒，只要不泄露，就万无一失。无需 2FA。 The entire issue is data leaks and phishing.

My Steam password is one short weird phrase that I can remember. I haven't changed it since high school, ~15 years ago. Never had any security issues.

The modern landscape is frustrating because that setup actually works. Passwords, from a technical perspective, are actually great and are are bulletproof as long as they don't leak. No 2FA required. The entire issue is data leaks and phishing.

在 HN 中查看
randusername 2026/03/24 00:44
这是一个比安全性更大的问题。

大规模的事件是不可避免的，但大规模的风险管理是一种仅附加的操作，最终会变得如此复杂，令人窒息的唯一办法就是不合规。

即使去看医生，我也恳求工作人员让我去看 PCP，而不是经历整个过程。现在需要 30 分钟才能完成开场审讯，内容涉及海外旅行、人口贩卖、疫苗意识、焦虑和抑郁小组、家庭暴力问题、有关最近跌倒的多部分问题以及他们不断提出的其他所有问题。通常一式三份，候诊室表格，护士的问题，医生的问题。

我知道每一个个人决定的背后都有一个恐怖故事，或者有人主动试图阻止一个故事，但总的来说，他们创造了自己的决定。

This is a much bigger problem than just security.

Incidents are inevitable at scale, but risk management at scale is an append-only operation that eventually becomes so complex and suffocating the only recourse is noncompliance.

Even going to the doctor I find myself pleading with the staff to just let me see my PCP instead of going through the full process. It takes 30 minutes now to get through the opening interrogation about overseas travel, human trafficking, vaccine awareness, anxiety and depression panels, domestic violence questions, multi-part questions about recent falls, and everything else that they keep tacking on. Usually in triplicate, waiting room forms, questions from the nurse, questions from the doctor.

And I know behind each of these individual decisions there is a horror story or someone proactively trying to prevent one, but altogether they create their own.

在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/03/23 23:56 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
kstenerud 2026/03/24 00:36
现在我们正处于下一级别的安全疲劳的门槛：权限疲劳。

令人震惊的是，很少有人关注即将到来的安全噩梦。对于一个坏人来说，毒害人工智能会话并不需要太多时间，等待你开始选择是，是，是，然后溜进一些不好的东西。

And now we're at the threshold of the next level of security fatigue: permission fatigue.

It's shocking how little people are paying attention to this upcoming security nightmare. It wouldn't take much for a bad actor to poison an AI session to wait for you to start selecting yes, yes, yes and then slip in something bad.

在 HN 中查看
gz5 2026/03/23 23:40
绝对地。说起来容易做起来难，但最好的安全是结构安全——对最终用户来说尽可能接近隐形。在我看来，这需要成为目标，即使不能完全实现。
Absolutely. Easier said than done, but the best security is structural security - as near to invisible for end users as possible. This needs to be the goal, imo, even if not fully achievable.
在 HN 中查看
ctxc 2026/03/23 23:42
相当明显吗？或者不是每个人都这样？
Fairly obvious? Or isn't it that way for everyone?
在 HN 中查看
Lerc 2026/03/24 00:09
非常明显，但看似显而易见的事情实际上可能并非如此。 值得验证。

如果需要改变，让组织根据显而易见的事情采取行动比您想象的要困难。 有研究指出并说你做错了事，现在却被告知，就像快速地打开和关闭灯，并用一种​​令人毛骨悚然的声音呻吟“责任”一样。

Very obvious, but things that seem obvious might not actually be true. It is worth verifying.

Getting organisations to act on the obvious if it requires changing is harder than you might think. Having research to point to and saying you are doing the wrong thing and now you've been told is like turning the lights on and off really quickly and moaning "Liability" in a spooky voice.

在 HN 中查看
ctxc 2026/03/24 00:18
很公平。我很难提倡良好的密码流程，因为“标准”规定频繁轮换等。

说实话，当你 申请 这些标准具有背景，并且面对人们最低限度地指着这些标准，有时你会显得知识渊博——这就是研究/标准的权威。

不管怎样，我浏览了你的个人资料并学到了一个新词，milquetoast - 所以谢谢你！

Fair enough. I had a hard time advocating for good password flows because "standards" said frequent rotation etc.

And tbh when you apply those standards with context and are faced with people bare-minimum pointing at the standards, you sometimes come off as less knowledgeable - such is the authority of research/standards.

Anyway, I skimmed your profile and learnt a new word, milquetoast - so thanks for that!

在 HN 中查看
languagehacker 2026/03/23 23:58
很高兴在这里见到纽约州立大学奥尔巴尼分校！
Nice to see SUNY Albany on here!
在 HN 中查看
kotaKat 2026/03/24 00:27
有时，我需要向公司 IT 部门索要每个提升请求的理由日志。我确信我已经提交了至少几百个“因为我这么说”和至少三个蜜蜂电影剧本。
At some point I need to ask Corporate IT for my justification logs for every elevation request. I'm certainly sure I've submitted at least a couple hundred "because I said so"s and at least three Bee Movie scripts.
在 HN 中查看
scuff3d 2026/03/24 00:21
昨天和一个人讨论这个问题。从冷启动开始，为了进入虚拟机，我必须在其上进行实际工作

1.输入密码解密电脑

2. 输入用户名和密码登录我的帐户

3. 输入另一组凭据以访问公司 VPN

4. 输入另一个用户名和密码以访问虚拟机所在的网络

5. 输入另一个用户名和密码以访问实际机器

6. 然后导航 docker/git/etc 的一系列授权以实际执行任何有用的操作

Was talking with someone about this yesterday. From cold start, for me to get to the VM I do my actual work on I have to

1. Enter a password to decrypt the computer

2. Enter a username and password to log into my account

3. Enter another set of credentials to access the corporate VPN

4. Enter another username and password to access the network the VM is on

5. Enter another username and password to get to the actual machine

6. And then navigate a nest of authorization for docker/git/etc to actually do anything useful

在 HN 中查看
BoneShard 2026/03/24 01:50
业余爱好者。 JIT 门户在哪里 - 可以提交票证以访问生产虚拟机？
Amateurs. Where is a JIT portal - to raise a ticket in order to access prod VMs?
在 HN 中查看
nathan_compton 2026/03/24 00:08
我必须“单点登录”的次数确实令人抓狂。
The number of times I have to "single sign on" is truly maddening.
在 HN 中查看
HansHamster 2026/03/24 03:45
至少你可以勾选“保持登录”复选框，然后……几个小时后就会被踢出，并收到一条自鸣得意的“你已成功退出”消息。
At least you can tick the "stay signed in" checkbox and... get kicked out a few hours later with a smug "you successfully signed out" message.
在 HN 中查看
lloydatkinson 2026/03/24 00:51
谁能想到每天向手机发送 20 次 2FA、3FA、MFA 请求会导致用户疲劳！

一些个人亮点分布在多项工作中：

- IT 部门决定通过组策略将一些糟糕的 SharePoint 页面设为 Chrome 的浏览器主页。该页面要求您登录您的 Microsoft 帐户。如果是星期一早上，您必须通过短信进行身份验证才能查看您的主页，或者，我通常会忽略它。每次我打开新的浏览器选项卡时，我都会收到一条新短信。这种情况一次持续了几周，出于恶意，每天可能会发送 50 条短信。最终他们禁用了那个垃圾。任何处理过微软登录的人都知道，“记住我”几乎完全是一个虚假的选项，没有任何故意的作用。 [1]

- 需要登录您的 Microsoft 帐户的 VPN，然后该帐户会向您发送一条通知到 Microsoft Authenticator 应用程序，该应用程序需要进行面部扫描，然后输入代码，然后进行另一次面部扫描。在设计过程中，从来没有人认为输入代码是多余的。

- 尽管我是一名软件工程师，能够随意生成可执行的二进制文件，这些二进制文件似乎都受到我们的安全软件的信任，但我仍然需要每月与 IT 人员交谈 5 次，以获得安全软件批准的<非常流行的众所周知的广泛开发工具>。

- 前一个的优点是，我经常需要手动提供上面使用的确切 DLL。每次更新都意味着新的文件哈希，这意味着再次重复。

- 我的工作计算机的本地管理员权限，但出于某种原因，它让我们输入密码才能打开 Windows 任务管理器。

- 告诉我们他们已经购买了我们应该使用的 Copilot 许可证，只是 IT 在使用后几乎立即给您打电话，因为他们的企业垃圾防火墙开始对 Copilot 向 github.com 的请求发起攻击，尽管我们已经在使用 GitHub。

[1]： https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20150415-the-buttons-that...

Who could have guess bombarding users with 2FA, 3FA, MFA requests to their phone 20 times a day would cause fatigue!

Some personal highlights spread across multiple jobs:

- IT decided they'd make some awful SharePoint page the browser homepage for Chrome via group policy. That page required you to login to your Microsoft account. If it was a Monday morning you'd have to authenticate via SMS just to see your homepage, or, what I did usually was ignore it. Every time I opened a new browser tab I'd get a new SMS. This went on for weeks at a time, maybe 50 SMS per day, out of spite. Eventually they disabled that crap. Anyone that deals with Microsoft logins knows that "Remember me" is almost totally a fake option that does nothing on purpose. [1]

- VPN that requires logging into your Microsoft account, which then sends you a notification to Microsoft Authenticator app, which requires a face scan, followed by typing in a code, followed by another face scan. At no point in the design process of that did someone think typing the code was redundant.

- Despite being a software engineer, able to produce executable binaries at will, which all seem to be trusted by our security software, I still need to talk to IT maybe 5 times a month to get <very popular well known widespread development tool> approved by the security software.

- Bonus points for the previous one, I often need to manually provide the exact DLL's used by the above. Every update means new file hashes, meaning repeating it all over again.

- Local admin rights to my work machine and yet for whatever reason IT make us type a password to open Windows Task Manager.

- Telling us all they have bought Copilot licenses we should use, only for IT to ring you almost immediately after using it because their corpo-garbage firewall starts throwing a fit about Copilot's requests to github.com, despite us already using GitHub.

[1]: https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20150415-the-buttons-that...

在 HN 中查看
GoblinSlayer 2026/03/24 01:36
> 经安全软件批准。

哈哈，与 netcat 一起度过了这一刻（因为它可以被 haxorz 使用！111）

> approved by the security software.

lol, had this moment with netcat (because it can be used by haxorz!111)

在 HN 中查看
general_reveal 2026/03/24 00:00
尽可能多地离开这些平台。这大概是您能获得的唯一安全保障。如果你还在黑客帝国里，听听这里的怪人把“不要相信任何东西”认真到了荒谬的地步。

《黑客帝国》并不是虚构的。我们的现代互联网 是 一个系统。你必须弄清楚如何真正摆脱它而生活，因为它绝对拥有你。

__

启示录 13:16-17

“他使所有人，无论大小、富人还是穷人、自由人还是奴隶，都在他们的右手或额头上接受一个标记： 并且除了有标记的人之外，没有人可以买卖……”

Just get off as many of these platform as you can. That’s about the only security that you’ll ever get. If you are still in the Matrix, listen the weirdos on here that take “don’t trust anything” seriously to the point of absurdity.

The Matrix was not fiction. Our modern internet is a system. You have to figure out how to live truly free from it, because it absolutely owns you.

__

Revelation 13:16–17

“And he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads: And that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark…”
